stem: Choice B: Skeletal muscle hyperplasia—the generation of new muscle fibers—is not a recognized physiologic response to training in adult humans. Most strength gains and muscle growth in resistance training come from hypertrophy, where the size of individual fibers increases. Satellite cells do become activated in response to mechanical load and help support hypertrophy by donating nuclei, but they do not cause new fiber formation. While animal models may demonstrate some hyperplasia, human muscle fibers respond to training primarily by getting larger, not by increasing in number. Given this, the histologic finding of hyperplasia is not expected in this athlete. Choice D: These are hallmark changes seen in endurance-trained muscle. With prolonged aerobic activity, the body adapts by enhancing oxygen delivery and usage—achieved through capillary angiogenesis and increased mitochondrial biogenesis. These changes allow slow-twitch (type I) fibers to sustain activity over time with greater efficiency. However, this athlete’s training is anaerobic in nature, prioritizing strength and power rather than oxidative endurance. Type II (fast-twitch) fibers dominate such activity, and they do not adapt by increasing mitochondria or capillaries.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This adolescent presents with symptoms consistent with slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), a disorder in which the femoral head slips posteriorly and inferiorly through the growth plate (physis), typically during the rapid growth of early adolescence. Common presenting features include a subacute or chronic limp, dull pain in the hip, groin, or knee, and decreased internal rotation of the hip. On physical examination, flexing the hip often causes obligate external rotation, and patients may exhibit an antalgic or waddling gait. These findings, particularly in an overweight male in the 10–13 age range, are highly suggestive of SCFE. The most important risk factor for SCFE is obesity. Increased body mass places excessive mechanical stress on the physis, especially during periods of accelerated growth when the growth plate is biomechanically weaker due to hormonal changes. The cartilage matrix becomes more vulnerable under these conditions, allowing slippage to occur. In addition, obesity-associated metabolic factors—such as insulin resistance and altered leptin signaling—may further disrupt normal growth plate function and contribute to instability. SCFE is diagnosed clinically and confirmed radiographically. Anteroposterior and frog-leg lateral X-rays of the pelvis typically reveal posterior and medial displacement of the femoral epiphysis relative to the femoral neck. A classic radiographic description is the appearance of “ice cream slipping off the cone,” which illustrates how the femoral head seems to slide off the metaphysis. Although obesity increases the risk of SCFE, the diagnosis can still occur in adolescents who are not overweight. The most consistent feature across affected patients is age, as SCFE almost always presents in children between 10 and 13 years old. When a younger child presents with a painful limp, typically under the age of 9, other conditions such as Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease should be considered instead. Regardless of weight, SCFE should be suspected in any adolescent with a painful gait and limited internal rotation of the hip. The pathologic process involves displacement of the epiphysis of the femoral head across the growth plate. Treatment involves urgent surgical pinning in situ to prevent further slippage and complications such as avascular necrosis or chondrolysis. Long-term follow-up is important, including weight management and monitoring of the contralateral hip, which is at risk for developing the same condition. A 13-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent right knee pain that has progressively worsened over the past month. He says the pain began after he tripped during basketball practice, but there was no fall or direct trauma. Since then, he has been avoiding sports and complains of intermittent pain that radiates to the thigh. His mother reports that he has been less active recently and appears to be limping, especially at the end of the day. Past medical history is notable for childhood asthma, managed with inhaled corticosteroids, and a learning disability for which he attends special classes. He was born at term by cesarean section due to breech presentation. His diet consists largely of processed foods, and he spends several hours daily playing video games. On examination, his BMI is at the 98th percentile. He walks with an antalgic gait, and there is limited internal rotation of the right hip. options.E: E. Sedentary lifestyle Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Breech presentation is a known risk factor for developmental dysplasia of the hip (DDH), particularly in female infants. In DDH, the femoral head is unstable or dislocated from the acetabulum due to abnormal intrauterine positioning, and it usually presents during infancy or early childhood. Diagnosis is confirmed by ultrasound or X-ray, and treatment involves bracing or surgical correction. Although both DDH and SCFE involve hip pathology, they have different pathophysiology, age of onset, and risk profiles. Breech positioning is unrelated to the development of SCFE in adolescents. Choice C: Prolonged systemic corticosteroid use is strongly associated with avascular necrosis (AVN) due to its role in disrupting the microcirculation of the femoral head and impairing bone turnover. AVN often presents with deep-seated hip pain and can lead to collapse of the femoral head if untreated. However, inhaled corticosteroids used for asthma management, as in this case, are not systemically absorbed in amounts sufficient to affect bone vasculature. Moreover, AVN is distinct from SCFE in terms of radiographic findings, typical age group, and biomechanical etiology. Choice D: Learning disabilities may be associated with endocrinopathies or syndromic conditions (e.g., hypothyroidism, Down syndrome, Prader-Willi syndrome) that can predispose to SCFE. However, not all individuals with learning difficulties have these conditions. In the absence of clinical signs of hypothyroidism, such as fatigue, constipation, delayed growth, or cold intolerance, or dysmorphic features suggestive of a genetic syndrome, the presence of a learning disability alone is insufficient to implicate it as the primary risk factor in this case. Choice E: A sedentary lifestyle can lead to deconditioning, obesity, and poor musculoskeletal strength, indirectly increasing the risk for orthopedic problems. However, SCFE specifically results from increased mechanical stress across the hypertrophic zone of the femoral growth plate, leading to epiphyseal displacement. While this child’s screen time may contribute to weight gain, it is the elevated BMI that directly increases the axial load on the growth plate and is the key predisposing factor. Thus, inactivity is a contributing factor but not the most significant one. Bottom line: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) typically affects obese adolescent boys and presents with progressive hip or referred knee pain, limited hip motion, and an antalgic gait. The most significant risk factor is a high body mass index (BMI), which increases shear stress across the growth plate. Diagnosis is confirmed by imaging, and the definitive management is urgent surgical stabilization with in situ pinning to prevent further slippage and complications such as avascular necrosis. SCFE results from displacement of the femoral epiphysis relative to the femoral neck at the growth plate and typically affects preadolescent children aged 10–13 years. The condition is more common in boys and often presents as progressive hip, groin, or knee pain with a limp. Physical exam findings include decreased internal rotation and abduction, leg shortening, and external rotation. X-ray typically shows posterior and inferior displacement of the femoral head on the neck (“ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance). Early recognition is essential because untreated SCFE may lead to avascular necrosis and early osteoarthritis. The primary treatment is surgical pinning in situ to stabilize the femoral head and prevent progression. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. This patient presents with classic findings of osteoarthritis (OA) — a degenerative joint disease characterized by progressive cartilage erosion due to mechanical stress and aging. The knee pain is chronic and activity-related, in the setting of high BMI, and physical examination shows bony hypertrophy of the DIPs, a classic OA feature. The synovial fluid is non-inflammatory with <1,000 WBC/μL, further supporting OA over an inflammatory process. X-ray findings of asymmetric joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis are also pathognomonic. In OA, the initial insult is repetitive joint use and biomechanical stress that leads to activation of chondrocytes, which secrete pro-inflammatory mediators (e.g., IL-1, TNF-α) and matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs). These degrade type II collagen and proteoglycans in the cartilage matrix. Eventually, cartilage is worn away, and the underlying subchondral bone becomes exposed. As the bone remodels under stress, it develops a dense, ivory-like appearance called "eburnation," and osteophytes may form at joint margins. This degenerative cycle results in pain, reduced mobility, and stiffness that worsens with use. Unlike autoimmune arthropathies, OA has no systemic inflammation, and inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) remain normal. Importantly, OA of the hands affects the distal interphalangeal joints (DIPs), known as Heberden nodes, in addition to the proximal interphalangeal joints (PIPs), referred to as Bouchard nodes. This is in contrast to rheumatoid arthritis (RA), which typically spares the DIPs and instead involves the MCPs and PIPs. A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressively worsening bilateral knee pain that began over three years ago. She describes the pain as dull and aching, most noticeable after walking or prolonged standing. She denies morning stiffness, systemic symptoms, or joint redness. Her past medical history includes obesity and venous stasis. On exam, she has crepitus with passive knee motion and visible bony enlargement of the distal interphalangeal joints. No erythema or warmth is present. Her BMI is 42 kg/m². Synovial fluid aspirated from the right knee is clear with a WBC count of 950/μL and normal glucose levels. A plain radiograph shows asymmetric joint space narrowing and evidence of subchondral sclerosis. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology of this patient's condition?

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. The provided X-ray shows the right femoral head displaced posteriorly and inferiorly relative to the neck of the femur. This misalignment is consistent with slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) and classically described as the “ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance. SCFE is a disorder of the proximal femoral physis (growth plate) that typically affects preadolescent and early adolescent males between the ages of 10 and 14. During this period, rapid growth weakens the hypertrophic zone of the physis, making it susceptible to shear stress. In SCFE, the epiphysis remains in the acetabulum, while the femoral metaphysis displaces anteriorly and superiorly. This results in a relative posterior and inferior slip of the epiphysis. The most consistent risk factor is age, though obesity, male sex, and African ancestry are commonly associated. Endocrine abnormalities (e.g., hypothyroidism, growth hormone deficiency) and renal osteodystrophy are less common but important secondary risk factors. Patients often present with insidious onset of vague hip, groin, or thigh pain, and many report knee pain, which can mislead the diagnosis. Pain is usually progressive and worsens with activity. Affected children may develop limping, externally rotated gait, and limited internal rotation of the hip. Morning stiffness may occur due to joint irritation. Notably, trauma is absent or minimal in most cases. SCFE should be suspected in any child or adolescent with hip or knee pain, especially with a limp, even in the absence of obesity. On physical examination, patients often display obligate external rotation of the leg, especially with passive hip flexion. This is a compensatory response to maintain femoral head congruency. The gait is antalgic, and hip range of motion is usually limited, particularly in internal rotation and abduction. Neurologic examination is normal unless a complication like avascular necrosis has occurred. — Diagnosis is confirmed by anteroposterior and frog-leg lateral X-rays of the pelvis, which show displacement of the femoral epiphysis. Bilateral imaging is important, as SCFE is bilateral in up to 40% of cases. The classic radiologic sign is the posteroinferior slip of the epiphysis with a line (Klein’s line) failing to intersect the epiphysis on AP view. Prompt recognition is essential to prevent long-term complications. The definitive treatment is immediate surgical stabilization with in situ single screw pinning of the epiphysis. This prevents further displacement and reduces the risk of complications such as avascular necrosis, chondrolysis, premature osteoarthritis, and leg length discrepancy. Weight-bearing should be restricted until surgical fixation is completed. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. Progressively limited finger extension in an older man with intact sensation and pulses, long-standing diabetes, and chronic alcohol use, together with palpable cords in the palm and the lesion shown in the image, indicates Dupuytren contracture—a fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar fascia that produces flexion contractures of the ulnar digits. Clinical presentation is insidious, with stiffness, morning tightness, and functional difficulty placing the hand flat on a table (positive tabletop test). The deformity is constant, and unlike trigger finger, there is no intermittent snapping or sudden release. Pain is minimal, neurologic symptoms are absent, and the disability arises from a fixed flexion posture rather than entrapment neuropathy or stenosing tenosynovitis. Pathogenesis centers on proliferation of fibroblasts and myofibroblasts within the pretendinous bands of the palmar aponeurosis, driven by profibrotic mediators (eg, TGF-β) and increased type III collagen and fibronectin deposition. Myofibroblast contraction forms nodules and longitudinal cords that progressively shorten the fascia and tether the MCP and PIP joints—most often the ring and little fingers—producing irreversible contracture if untreated. The process is a localized fibroproliferative diathesis rather than an inflammatory arthritis, which explains the paucity of warmth, erythema, or systemic features. Risk is strongly influenced by ancestry and comorbidity. Northern European—classically Norwegian—descent confers a high hereditary predisposition. Alcoholism is the most important modifiable association and correlates with earlier onset and more aggressive disease. Diabetes mellitus increases prevalence via nonenzymatic glycation–linked collagen changes and microvascular factors. Epilepsy is another recognized association, historically linked to anticonvulsant exposure and shared fibrotic pathways. Additional contributors include male sex, age >50, smoking, manual-vibration exposure, and related fibromatoses (eg, plantar fibromatosis, Peyronie disease). Management is staged by functional impairment and joint angles. Mild disease without contracture is observed; hand therapy and nighttime extension splints maintain motion but do not reverse cords. Early, painful nodules may respond to intralesional corticosteroids. For functional contracture, options include collagenase Clostridium histolyticum injection to enzymatically rupture the cord, percutaneous needle aponeurotomy (needle fasciotomy) to divide cords through small punctures, or limited/open fasciectomy for dense, recurrent, or PIP-dominant disease. Intervention is typically recommended for MCP contracture ≥30° or any PIP contracture that interferes with function. Recurrence is common—higher in younger patients, strong diathesis, and PIP involvement—so postoperative therapy, splinting in extension, and longitudinal follow-up are essential.

Choice B: A deficiency in osteoblast function or recruitment leads to impaired bone formation, as seen in conditions like osteogenesis imperfecta or certain forms of osteoporosis. Such conditions would be characterized by decreased bone mass and potentially low or normal ALP levels depending on severity. In contrast, Paget disease is marked by excessive osteoblast activity—not a deficiency—due to the disorganized attempt to lay down new bone following increased resorption. The end result in Paget disease is thickened but structurally weak bone. Additionally, osteoblast deficiency would not explain the radiographic cotton-wool skull appearance or elevated ALP, both of which are seen in Paget disease. Choice C: Decreased osteoclast activity is characteristic of conditions like osteopetrosis, where bone resorption is impaired, resulting in dense, brittle bones. Patients with osteopetrosis often have pancytopenia due to encroachment of bony overgrowth into the marrow space, as well as cranial nerve compression due to narrowed foramina. This is the opposite mechanism of Paget disease, which begins with increased osteoclastic activity, not decreased. Additionally, osteopetrosis does not cause elevated ALP, skull enlargement, or the classic radiographic findings associated with Paget disease. Imaging shows a radiolucent nidus with surrounding sclerosis, not the diffuse layered periosteal reaction seen here. Histologically, it would show woven bone and osteoid, not sheets of uniform small round cells. The patient’s worsening, unrelieved pain and fever argue strongly against osteoid osteoma. Choice E: Osteomyelitis is a common cause of bone pain and fever in children, usually following trauma or bacteremia. It typically affects the metaphysis, not the diaphysis, due to slower blood flow and capillary loops. Histology would show inflammatory cells and necrosis, not uniform small blue cells. Imaging usually shows focal lytic lesions without the layered periosteal reaction seen here. Lack of overlying erythema or warmth and the subacute course with minimal systemic toxicity also argue against infection. Bottom line: Ewing sarcoma is a diaphyseal malignant bone tumor of childhood that mimics osteomyelitis but has characteristic radiographic and histologic features. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient presents with proximal muscle weakness, elevated CK, and a biopsy consistent with a congenital myopathy—findings that localize the problem to the muscle fiber and contractile apparatus. One of the diagnostic techniques used is electron microscopy, which allows visualization of sarcomere structure and its changes during contraction. Sarcomeres are the functional units of skeletal muscle and contain several key structural bands. During contraction, thin filaments (actin) slide past thick filaments (myosin), causing the sarcomere to shorten. However, not all bands change in size during this process. The A-band, which corresponds to the full length of the myosin filaments, remains unchanged in length during contraction because the length of the thick filaments themselves does not vary. This observation is crucial in understanding muscle physiology. The shortening of the sarcomere occurs due to increased overlap of actin and myosin, not because the filaments themselves contract. A 25-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive muscle fatigue and weakness over the past few months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs, rising from a seated position, and lifting objects above his head. His symptoms are symmetrical and more pronounced in the proximal muscles. He has no sensory deficits, no fasciculations, and deep tendon reflexes are preserved. Laboratory studies reveal mildly elevated creatine kinase levels, and electromyography demonstrates a myopathic pattern. Muscle biopsy shows fiber size variation and increased central nuclei. Electron microscopy reveals that certain regions of the sarcomere shorten during contraction, while others, specifically one band, maintain a constant length throughout. Which of the following labeled regions on the image remains unchanged in length during muscle contraction?
learning_summary: The correct answer is B

options.E: 7 Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The sarcomere is defined as the distance between two Z-lines and represents the full contractile unit of muscle. During contraction, actin filaments slide toward the M-line, reducing the sarcomere length. This is the physical basis for muscle shortening and contraction. Therefore, the sarcomere does not maintain a fixed length. If this region were unaffected during contraction, muscle shortening would not be possible. Choice C: The M-band is the central part of the A-band, composed of proteins that help stabilize and cross-link thick myosin filaments. While the M-line itself doesn't "shorten" like the I-band or H-zone, it is not what the question is asking about. The A-band, not the M-band, is the correct answer because it spans the full length of the thick filaments and remains constant. The M-band is just a central anchor and does not represent the full unchanging band. Choice D: The I-band contains only thin actin filaments and spans from the end of one A-band to the beginning of another across a Z-line. During contraction, actin filaments slide over myosin, reducing the width of the I-band. This is one of the key changes visible during sarcomere shortening. The I-band shortens and does not remain constant in length. This eliminates it as the correct answer. Choice E: The H-zone is the region in the middle of the A-band that contains only thick (myosin) filaments with no actin overlap. As contraction proceeds and actin filaments slide inward, the H-zone becomes narrower or may disappear entirely. This change reflects the dynamic overlap between actin and myosin. Because the H-zone does not remain constant during contraction, it cannot be the correct answer. Its narrowing is a hallmark of active sarcomere shortening. Bottom line: The A-band represents the full length of thick myosin filaments and does not change in length during contraction. The I-band (actin only), H-zone (myosin only), and sarcomere length all shorten during contraction. Histology Musculoskeletal (Miscellaneous) None

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. Cervical spondylosis refers to age-related degeneration of the cervical spine and is a common cause of chronic neck pain in older adults, particularly those over age 50. The condition involves disc degeneration, osteophyte formation, and sometimes narrowing of the neural foramina or spinal canal. This patient’s presentation of progressive, non-radiating neck pain with stiffness after inactivity and MRI showing degenerative vertebral changes strongly aligns with cervical spondylosis. Importantly, cervical spondylosis can present with or without neurologic findings. In this case, the absence of radicular symptoms or neurologic deficits suggests a milder form limited to mechanical degeneration. The finding of neural foraminal narrowing without spinal cord compression supports this. The chronicity and pattern of stiffness (brief, improving with movement) also favor a degenerative mechanical origin rather than an inflammatory condition. While spondylosis can occur in the lumbar spine, the cervical region is more commonly tested on USMLE. The condition involves degeneration of the vertebral body, including the pars interarticularis. A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic due to several months of neck pain that has gradually worsened. He describes the discomfort as a dull, aching sensation in the posterior neck that sometimes radiates to the shoulders, particularly after long periods of desk work or driving. He denies any trauma, recent infections, or constitutional symptoms. He also reports occasional stiffness in the morning lasting about 10–15 minutes but says it improves throughout the day. There is no history of numbness, weakness, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. He has well-controlled hypertension and takes lisinopril. Neurological examination is normal, with preserved strength and sensation in all extremities. Reflexes are symmetric. MRI of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes involving the vertebral bodies and intervertebral discs, with osteophyte formation and mild narrowing of the neural foramina at multiple levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
learning_summary: The correct answer is B

options.D: Herniated cervical disc Choice A: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) typically affects young men under the age of 40 and presents with chronic inflammatory back pain that improves with exercise and worsens with rest. It commonly involves the sacroiliac joints and lumbar spine, with progressive stiffness and eventual spinal fusion if untreated. The cervical spine may be affected in
options.E: Rheumatoid arthritis later stages, but it is not the usual initial site. Additionally, AS often presents with extra-articular features such as uveitis, enthesitis, or limited chest expansion, none of which are seen in this patient. The brief morning stiffness described is mechanical in nature, not the prolonged inflammatory stiffness of AS. Furthermore, imaging in AS shows sacroiliitis and syndesmophyte formation, not isolated degenerative changes with osteophytes as seen here. Choice C: Cervical strain, or neck muscle strain, is usually caused by an acute event such as a whiplash injury, poor posture, or muscle overuse. It typically presents with localized neck pain, muscle tenderness, and reduced range of motion due to pain, often without associated radiographic abnormalities. In this patient, the pain has been gradually progressive over months with no mention of trauma or muscular tenderness, making acute strain less likely. Additionally, the presence of degenerative changes such as osteophyte formation and foraminal narrowing on MRI cannot be explained by a simple muscle strain. The age of the patient and chronicity of symptoms further favor a degenerative etiology over a soft tissue injury. Choice D: A herniated cervical disc usually presents with radiculopathy, such as sharp or burning pain radiating down the arm, numbness, paresthesia, or weakness in a dermatomal distribution. In more severe cases, spinal cord compression may result in myelopathy, presenting with gait disturbances, hyperreflexia, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. However, this patient’s neurologic exam is entirely normal, with no signs of sensory or motor deficits. Additionally, MRI findings in herniated disc cases typically show focal protrusion or extrusion of disc material impinging on the nerve root or cord, rather than diffuse vertebral degeneration and osteophyte formation. The absence of compressive symptoms or signs rules out a clinically significant disc herniation. Choice E: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an autoimmune inflammatory disease that classically affects middle-aged women with symmetric small joint involvement (e.g., MCP, PIP joints of the hands). Cervical spine involvement does occur, particularly at the atlantoaxial joint, and can be serious when present. However, it is rarely the initial or isolated manifestation. This patient lacks typical systemic features of RA, such as joint swelling, prolonged morning stiffness (>30 minutes), or elevated inflammatory markers. Additionally, imaging findings in RA would show erosions, joint space narrowing, and possibly pannus formation, not osteophyte development and multilevel degenerative disc changes. The demographics, clinical picture, and imaging all argue against RA. Bottom line: Cervical spondylosis is a common degenerative condition in older adults presenting with chronic neck pain. MRI findings often show disc space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and foraminal narrowing. USMLE may test this diagnosis when a patient >50 presents with neck pain and degenerative cervical spine findings without neurologic deficits. answer_text: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Anterior dislocations are much less common than posterior and typically result from trauma involving abduction and external rotation forces on the hip. The limb in such cases appears externally rotated and slightly flexed, often without the significant shortening seen in posterior dislocations. In this case, the physical exam shows internal rotation and shortening, which contradicts the expected findings for anterior dislocation. Although the injury mechanism is traumatic, the leg position is not compatible with anterior dislocation. Choice B: Femoral neck fractures are common in older adults due to osteoporosis but may also occur in younger patients after high-energy trauma. They typically present with a shortened and externally rotated leg. However, this patient's age and mechanism of injury make posterior dislocation more likely. Additionally, femoral neck fractures usually do not cause the marked internal rotation seen here. The absence of bony deformity or crepitus further argues against this diagnosis. Choice C: Intertrochanteric fractures also tend to produce shortened and externally rotated legs. They are typically seen in elderly patients and result from falls, not high-velocity trauma. The presence of internal rotation here contradicts this possibility. Moreover, intertrochanteric fractures would likely show local tenderness, swelling, or crepitus, none of which are described. The clinical scenario strongly favors a dislocation rather than a fracture. Choice E: SCFE occurs in adolescents, typically obese males aged 10–16, due to shear stress at the growth plate. It causes chronic hip or knee pain and limp, rather than acute post-traumatic symptoms. Physical exam usually reveals external rotation of the affected leg, not internal. The age of this patient (27 years) and the mechanism (MVA) do not fit with SCFE. This diagnosis can be ruled out based on demographics and presentation alone. Bottom line: Posterior hip dislocation is the most common type and presents with a shortened, internally rotated leg. Often occurs in dashboard trauma scenarios, requiring prompt reduction to prevent avascular necrosis. USMLE tests leg position as a key diagnostic clue to distinguish dislocations and fractures. explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient presents with localized lateral wrist pain in the context of repetitive hand use while breastfeeding, most notably worsened by the Finkelstein maneuver, which strongly suggests de Quervain tenosynovitis. The condition is characterized by inflammation of the tendon sheaths of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis muscles, which pass through the first dorsal compartment of the wrist. These tendons facilitate thumb abduction and extension, and inflammation leads to pain with repetitive thumb movements, especially in postpartum women due to frequent lifting and wrist deviation. The Finkelstein test is highly specific and was clearly positive in this case. It involves placing the thumb inside a closed fist and ulnar deviating the wrist. The radial-sided pain reproduced by this maneuver, combined with the patient’s risk factor (breastfeeding with repetitive wrist motion), confirms the diagnosis. A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening pain in her right wrist for the past 6 days. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the outer (thumb- side) part of the wrist, and says it has made it increasingly difficult to carry out certain tasks like picking up her newborn daughter, who is 7 weeks old. The discomfort started mildly but has now progressed to the point where even turning a doorknob or lifting a coffee mug causes discomfort. She denies numbness, tingling, trauma, fever, or joint swelling. Her medical history is unremarkable. She is exclusively breastfeeding and has recently increased the frequency and duration of feedings. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the lateral (radial) wrist, just proximal to the base of the thumb. There is no visible swelling or redness, and full passive

Patients often report nocturnal paresthesias, hand clumsiness, and sometimes thenar muscle atrophy. This patient's symptoms are purely mechanical, with no sensory disturbance, motor weakness, or nocturnal worsening. Moreover, the pain is localized to the radial styloid, not the volar aspect of the wrist, and is provoked by a positive Finkelstein maneuver, which implicates tendon sheath inflammation rather than nerve entrapment. Choice C: Inflammation of the wrist joint capsule—whether due to synovitis, arthritis, or joint effusion—typically causes diffuse wrist pain, visible swelling, and limited passive range of motion, especially in cases of inflammatory arthritis. This patient, however, has no swelling, normal passive ROM, and pain that is sharply elicited by a specific maneuver involving the thumb and wrist (Finkelstein test). The pain is also focal over the first dorsal compartment, rather than generalized to the radiocarpal joint. Additionally, there is no morning stiffness or other signs suggesting joint inflammation. These features argue strongly against radiocarpal synovitis or capsulitis as the cause. Choice D: Overuse injuries involving flexor tendons typically affect the volar (palmar) side of the wrist or medial elbow (e.g., medial epicondylitis) and are provoked by wrist flexion, forearm pronation, or gripping activities. This patient’s pain is clearly located on the radial (lateral) aspect of the wrist and is exacerbated by thumb flexion and ulnar deviation, not flexion of the wrist or forearm muscles. Furthermore, the first dorsal compartment contains extensor tendons, not flexors, and the Finkelstein test targets this specific structure. This pattern of findings does not support a flexor-based overuse injury and makes this choice incorrect. Choice E: Although postpartum autoimmune flares can occur, this patient's symptoms are isolated, mechanical, and not migratory or systemic. Inflammatory conditions like rheumatoid arthritis or seronegative spondyloarthropathies typically present with multiple joint involvement, morning stiffness lasting >30–60 minutes, fatigue, or other systemic signs such as rash, weight loss, or uveitis. There are no constitutional symptoms, no bilateral or symmetric joint complaints, and no signs of synovitis or erosive disease. Her pain is provoked by a biomechanical maneuver and is well-localized, which is not typical for systemic inflammatory arthritides. Bottom line: De Quervain tenosynovitis is caused by inflammation of the tendon sheaths of the first dorsal compartment, especially in postpartum women. It presents with radial wrist pain exacerbated by Finkelstein’s maneuver. Pathology Musculoskeletal (Miscellaneous) None Subject System Topic

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient has classic findings of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), characterized by prolonged morning stiffness and symmetric involvement of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints, with sparing of the distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints. RA is a clinical diagnosis supported by serologies (positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies) and imaging findings such as joint space narrowing and marginal erosions, as seen in this patient. Synovial fluid analysis typically shows sterile inflammation (WBC 2,000–50,000/mm³, no crystals). Although synovial biopsy is not routinely performed, the hallmark histologic feature is synovial pannus, which consists of granulation tissue composed of fibroblasts, inflammatory cells, and neovascularization. This invasive tissue erodes cartilage and bone, driving the characteristic joint destruction in RA.

Choice C: These are hallmark findings of osteoarthritis (OA), a degenerative joint disease that typically affects weight-bearing joints and the hands, often involving the DIP and PIP joints in an asymmetric pattern. It usually does not cause prolonged morning stiffness or systemic inflammation. X-rays in OA reveal joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, subchondral sclerosis, and cysts, all of which result from chronic mechanical stress and cartilage degeneration. Choice D: Granulomas are typically associated with chronic infections (like tuberculosis) or sarcoidosis, not rheumatoid arthritis. Although rheumatoid nodules may exhibit central necrosis and palisading histiocytes (which resemble granulomas), the synovial membrane in RA shows dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrates and pannus formation, not classic granulomatous inflammation. Bottom line: Synovial pannus, a hyperplastic invasive tissue, is the hallmark histologic feature of rheumatoid arthritis and leads to progressive joint destruction. explanation: The correct answer is A. This patient presents with slowly progressive deep bone pain, worse at night, and imaging findings consistent with a lytic, calcified lesion involving the pelvis. The histopathology reveals neoplastic chondrocytes embedded in lacunae, a classic appearance for chondrosarcoma — a malignant cartilage-forming tumor seen in older adults. The histo image is extremely buzzy for USMLE. It often arises in flat bones like the pelvis, femur, or proximal humerus and can appear radiographically as lytic lesions with “rings and arcs” or stippled calcifications, which represent mineralized cartilage. Chondrosarcomas grow slowly but are locally aggressive and may cause cortical destruction or soft tissue extension. Grossly, they are described as having a glistening, bluish-gray appearance, due to the cartilage content. Histologically, they demonstrate malignant cartilage cells with hyperchromatic nuclei, variable mitoses, and cells embedded within lacunae in a cartilaginous matrix, exactly as seen in the image provided. Patients typically report persistent pain that worsens over time and may be severe at night. Unlike osteoid osteoma, the pain is not typically responsive to NSAIDs. MRI and CT help define the lesion, but diagnosis is confirmed via biopsy. Management often requires surgical resection, as these tumors tend to resist chemotherapy and radiation.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a rare clonal proliferative disorder characterized by the accumulation of pathologic Langerhans cells, which are specialized dendritic cells normally involved in antigen presentation in the skin and mucosa. This condition typically affects children and can present with lytic bone lesions, such as in the skull, mandible, or long bones, leading to localized pain and swelling. Other manifestations can include skin rashes, otitis media, and, in disseminated cases, systemic symptoms like fever and organ dysfunction. Histologically, these abnormal cells are positive for CD1a positive and display distinctive intracytoplasmic inclusions called Birbeck granules. Under electron microscopy, Birbeck granules appear as rod-shaped or tennis racquet–shaped structures unique to Langerhans cells and help confirm the diagnosis of LCH. These granules are believed to be involved in receptor-mediated endocytosis. A 4-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with a 3-month history of a painful, swollen skull lesion. CML does not cause lytic bone lesions or exhibit Birbeck granules. The peripheral smear shows a left-shifted myeloid series with basophilia. Choice D: Monocytic proliferation occurs in acute monocytic leukemia (AML-M5). These conditions present with fatigue, infections, and organomegaly but do not involve lytic skull lesions or Langerhans cell morphology. Nonsense diagnosis for USMLE. Choice E: Plasma cell dyscrasias, such as multiple myeloma, cause lytic bone lesions due to osteoclast activation. However, the biopsy would show sheets of plasma cells, not Langerhans cells, and serum electrophoresis would reveal a monoclonal gammopathy. Bottom line: LCH is the answer on USMLE for a patient with lytic lesions + Birbeck granules. The latter need not be tennis racquet-shaped and can be simply rod-shaped. While immune complexes can cause joint swelling and inflammation, their typical presentation is a more systemic, migratory polyarthritis, often accompanied by constitutional symptoms and involvement of multiple organ systems. Choice C: This defect is seen in disorders like Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, where impaired phagolysosome fusion leads to defective intracellular killing of pathogens. Patients with this disorder present with recurrent bacterial infections, partial albinism, and neuropathy. It does not cause the acute, localized, neutrophil-rich inflammation characteristic of a gout attack. The inability to clear pathogens leads to immunodeficiency, not the robust acute inflammation seen in gout. Choice E: T-cell mediated cytotoxicity is a hallmark of type IV hypersensitivity reactions and diseases such as contact dermatitis and type 1 diabetes mellitus, where CD8+ T lymphocytes directly kill target cells. In autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, T-cell activation leads to chronic synovial inflammation and pannus formation, rather than the acute neutrophil-rich response seen in gout. Clinically, T-cell driven diseases often present with slower, progressive joint destruction and systemic features (e.g., fatigue, subcutaneous nodules) rather than acute monoarticular pain and swelling.

This 12-year-old boy presents with a chronic limp, groin/thigh pain, restricted internal hip rotation, and a classic radiograph demonstrating the femoral head displaced posteriorly and medially from the neck — the so-called “ice cream slipping off the cone” appearance. These findings are high-yield for slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE), a disorder of the growth plate (physis) common in boys aged 10–13. USMLE wants you to recognize this X-ray and associate it with the pathophysiology: mechanical failure at the hypertrophic zone of the physis allows the epiphysis to displace. Obesity is often—but not always— a risk factor; USMLE questions may omit this clue to test age and radiologic interpretation instead. In fact, many Qs emphasize age over BMI, and even a normal-weight adolescent may have SCFE. This child’s stable vital signs, subacute pain, absence of systemic symptoms, and localized mechanical limitation of motion further support this diagnosis. Management includes surgical pinning to prevent progression and complications such as avascular necrosis or chondrolysis. Failure to diagnose early may result in long-term hip dysfunction and early osteoarthritis.

Choice C: Lambert-Eaton syndrome typically presents with proximal muscle weakness, especially in the hip and shoulder girdles, and often improves with repeated use (facilitation). It is also commonly associated with autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth or erectile dysfunction, none of which are present here. Furthermore, Lambert-Eaton is most often associated with paraneoplastic syndromes, especially small cell lung cancer. There is no mention of underlying malignancy or autonomic findings in this case. Muscle biopsy is typically normal or nonspecific in LEMS — not notable for rimmed vacuoles. Choice D: Polymyositis typically presents with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, such as difficulty rising from a chair or combing hair, but distal muscles are usually spared. It typically affects middle-aged adults, and patients may have elevated creatine kinase and autoantibodies. The weakness progresses over weeks to months and tends to respond to immunosuppressive therapy. On biopsy, one would expect endomysial mononuclear inflammation with CD8+ T cells, but not rimmed vacuoles. The presence of distal involvement and basophilic vacuoles in an older man makes polymyositis less likely. Choice E: Statin-induced necrotizing myopathy often presents with rapid onset of muscle weakness and markedly elevated CK levels in patients who are taking statins. This case lacks mention of statin use, and labs are reported to be normal, including CK. The histologic hallmark of this condition is muscle fiber necrosis with minimal inflammation, not rimmed vacuoles. Additionally, this type of myopathy progresses more quickly and may be associated with anti-HMGCR antibodies. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is A. This patient presents with chronic shoulder pain that worsens when lying on the affected side and during overhead activities. The physical exam shows a painful arc of movement and localized tenderness over the anterior and lateral shoulder. The plain radiograph reveals a radiopaque deposit (as indicated by the arrow) in the region of the supraspinatus tendon—findings consistent with calcific tendinitis of the shoulder. Calcific tendinitis involves deposition of hydroxyapatite crystals within tendons, most commonly the supraspinatus, which is part of the rotator cuff. It often affects middle-aged women and presents with subacute or chronic shoulder pain, exacerbated by shoulder abduction and lying on the affected side. Pain may worsen during the resorptive phase of the calcium deposit when inflammation is greatest. The supraspinatus muscle, innervated by the suprascapular nerve (C5–C6), is responsible for initiating the first 15° of shoulder abduction. Beyond that, the deltoid muscle, innervated by the axillary nerve, becomes the primary abductor up to approximately 90°. Finally, trapezius and serratus anterior muscles, innervated by the accessory nerve (CN XI) and long thoracic nerve, respectively, are needed for upward rotation of the scapula to allow full abduction beyond 90°. In calcific tendinitis, the inflamed or impinged supraspinatus tendon impairs the initiation and continuity of this sequence, causing pain and limitation during active abduction.
